title: Biases In News Reading
summary: A domain hub for reading headlines, breaking stories, threads, commentary, and corrections without letting vividness or repetition become evidence.
audience: Readers, students, moderators, journalists, teachers, and anyone who wants better media-literacy habits.
guidingQuestion: Is this story changing what I know, or mostly changing what feels available, repeated, tribal, or urgent?
scenario: Use this hub when a claim is moving quickly, emotionally, or repeatedly and you need to slow the jump from exposure to belief.

workflow.label: Separate exposure from evidence
workflow.text: Write what you actually learned from the item, then write what merely became more vivid or easier to recall.

workflow.label: Restore the denominator
workflow.text: Ask what base rate, comparison group, missing sample, or non-newsworthy background rate would change the interpretation.

workflow.label: Track correction quality
workflow.text: If a correction appears, check whether it replaces the old causal story or only subtracts the false claim.

watchFor: Anecdotes that feel like trends before the denominator appears.
Corrections that leave the original explanatory frame intact.
Claims that feel stronger because many outlets are repeating the same weak source.
Stories that make an opponent's motives feel obvious faster than the facts justify.
starterProtocol: Read the headline and write the implied causal story in one sentence.
Name the strongest missing denominator or comparison class.
Search for one disconfirming or context-widening source before sharing.
Use the media-narrative prompt only after you have pasted the exact claim or passage.

title: Biases In Teams And Meetings
summary: A hub for rooms where agreement, hierarchy, speed, and social comfort can quietly replace independent judgment.
audience: Managers, facilitators, leadership teams, boards, classrooms, and project groups.
guidingQuestion: Is the room converging because the case is strong, or because dissent has become socially expensive?
scenario: Use this hub before decisions, retrospectives, hiring discussions, strategy reviews, or any meeting where the group feels settled too quickly.

workflow.label: Collect independent first passes
workflow.text: Have people write forecasts, concerns, and preferred options before the highest-status voice frames the room.

workflow.label: Protect the strongest objection
workflow.text: Give the most plausible dissenting case a scheduled turn even when the room already feels aligned.

workflow.label: Separate outcome from process
workflow.text: In reviews, ask whether the decision process was good before deciding whether the result made it look good.

watchFor: Silence being counted as agreement.
A senior person's early view becoming the comparison point for every later comment.
Visible action being rewarded before diagnosis is finished.
Postmortems that explain wins as skill and losses as circumstance.
starterProtocol: Open with private written judgments, not discussion.
Ask for the best reason the current plan could fail.
Assign someone to track unshared information and missing alternatives.
Close by recording the decision rule, not just the decision.

title: Biases In Teaching And Learning
summary: A hub for classrooms, coaching, tutoring, and curriculum design where fluency, familiarity, and confidence can impersonate understanding.
audience: Teachers, professors, tutors, instructional designers, coaches, parents, and learners.
guidingQuestion: Can the learner use the idea in a new case, or only recognize it when the surrounding cues are familiar?
scenario: Use this hub when a lesson feels clear, a student sounds confident, or a group seems to understand before transfer has been tested.

workflow.label: Test explanation, not recognition
workflow.text: Ask for a step-by-step mechanism and a transfer case before counting fluency as mastery.

workflow.label: Make expert blind spots visible
workflow.text: Force yourself to name the missing bridge a novice would need, not the shortcut an expert can safely take.

workflow.label: Use retrieval as evidence
workflow.text: Prefer low-stakes retrieval, spacing, and application over the feeling that rereading made the concept familiar.

watchFor: Students nodding because the example is familiar, not because the structure is understood.
Teachers skipping steps that feel too obvious from the expert side.
Confidence rising after exposure but before retrieval or transfer.
Learners reporting understanding because admitting confusion is socially costly.
starterProtocol: Ask the learner to explain the mechanism without the original example.
Give one near-transfer and one far-transfer question.
Have the teacher list assumptions hidden in the explanation.
End with a short retrieval prompt scheduled for later.

title: Biases In Relationships
summary: A hub for friendships, families, couples, workplaces, and communities where self-protection, motive reading, and memory repair can bend interpretation.
audience: People handling conflict, trust repair, family decisions, mentoring, coaching, or emotionally loaded conversations.
guidingQuestion: Am I reading the other person, the situation, or my own need for the story to protect me?
scenario: Use this hub when a disagreement is becoming a character verdict, when old memories are getting recruited, or when one person's motives suddenly feel obvious.

workflow.label: Separate behavior from motive
workflow.text: Describe what happened in observable terms before choosing a motive, trait, or moral story.

workflow.label: Run the situation pass
workflow.text: List pressures, misunderstandings, incentives, fatigue, and constraints before treating the behavior as character.

workflow.label: Check self-protection
workflow.text: Ask what the current story lets you avoid admitting about your own role, need, or uncertainty.

watchFor: A single behavior becoming a global trait verdict.
Your own preference being treated as what any reasonable person would feel.
Bad intent feeling obvious before situational explanations have been tested.
Memory becoming cleaner and more self-protective with each retelling.
starterProtocol: Write the observable behavior without motive language.
Generate three plausible non-hostile explanations.
Name one fact that would change your interpretation.
Choose a next question that lowers heat rather than wins the story.

title: Biases In Product And UX
summary: A hub for product decisions, interfaces, pricing, defaults, metrics, and AI-assisted workflows where choice architecture can quietly manufacture preference.
audience: Product managers, designers, researchers, founders, growth teams, policy designers, and anyone building decision environments.
guidingQuestion: Is the interface helping users express a preference, or shaping the preference before they notice?
scenario: Use this hub when the menu, default, metric, recommendation, or comparison structure might be steering users more than informing them.

workflow.label: Audit the default
workflow.text: Treat the current default as one explicit option and ask what case it would need to win without preselection.

workflow.label: Strip the decoy and anchor
workflow.text: Re-run the comparison after removing irrelevant reference prices, dominated options, and first numbers.

workflow.label: Protect the target metric
workflow.text: Ask whether the proxy metric is improving the real user outcome or becoming the thing the team now optimizes.

watchFor: A default option being treated as consent or recommendation.
A third option making one of two main options look better without changing its real value.
Automation outputs becoming the baseline users stop checking.
Teams optimizing a visible metric after losing contact with the underlying experience.
starterProtocol: Name the user's actual decision, not the interface event.
List every cue that makes one option feel easier, safer, or more normal.
Remove one cue and ask whether the preference still holds.
Test the cleaner frame with a user who has not seen the original version.
